1: ʾĕlōhîm mišpāṭêkhā lĕmelek tēn — O God, give your justice to the king. The opening word ʾĕlōhîm frames a royal prayer: the king's justice must be divinely donated — it cannot be self-generated. Mišpāṭ (justice, judgment) must transfer from God to the royal line by gift, not by royal claim. The parallel: wĕṣidqātekhā lĕben-melek — and your righteousness to the king's son. Both justice and righteousness flow downward from God through the Davidic line.
2: yādîn ʿammĕkhā bĕṣedeq — may he judge your people with righteousness. The imperfects here function as jussives — petition rather than description. wĕʿăniyyêkhā bĕmišpāṭ — and your poor with justice. The hallmark of the ideal king is attention to the ʿānî (the poor, the afflicted) — a criterion that surfaces repeatedly through this psalm (vv4, 12-14). In ancient Near Eastern royal ideology, power is displayed in conquest; in Psalms 72, power is displayed in protecting the vulnerable.
3: yiśśĕʾû hārîm shālôm lāʿām — may the mountains bear peace for the people. The mountains as bearers of shālôm images creation responding to righteous kingship — cosmic benefit flowing from royal justice. The noun shālôm encompasses wholeness, prosperity, and communal wellbeing simultaneously; the land itself reflects the moral state of its king.
4: yishpōṭ ʿăniyyê-ʿām — may he defend the poor of the people. The triple focus through this psalm on ʿānî/ʾebyôn/dal (poor, needy, weak — vv2, 4, 12-14) distinguishes Ps 72 from ancient Near Eastern royal ideology where the king's power is displayed through conquest. Here power is displayed through protection. Wîshabbēr ʿôshēq — and crush the oppressor. Justice toward the poor and punishment of the oppressor are the same royal act.
5: yîrāʾûkhā ʿim-shāmesh — may they fear you as long as the sun endures. Yāraʾ (to fear, revere) — the reverence is directed toward God (khā, you), not the king. The king's entire reign functions to cultivate fear of God, not of himself. Lifnê yārēaḥ dôr dôrîm — before the moon, throughout all generations — both sun and moon mark the endless duration of the ideal reign.
6: yēred kĕmāṭār ʿal-gêz — may he come down like rain on mown grass. Gêz (first cutting, freshly mown grass) specifically refers to the crop after its first shearing — cut down, vulnerable, needing rain to regrow. The image captures the king's arrival as gentle, life-giving nourishment rather than martial force; he restores rather than harvests. kĕrĕbîbîm zarzîp ʾāreṣ — like showers that water the earth.
7: yiprāḥ bĕyāmāyw ṣaddîq — may the righteous flourish in his days. Pāraḥ (to blossom, sprout, flourish) is botanical — the righteous person as a plant coming into bloom under the right conditions. The ideal king creates conditions for righteousness to flourish; he is not merely a moral agent himself but a moral environment for others. wĕrōb shālôm ʿad-bĕlî yārēaḥ — and abundance of peace, until the moon is no more.
8: wĕyerd miyyām ʿad-yām — may he have dominion from sea to sea. The formula miyyām ʿad-yām became a standard expression of universal sovereignty; Zechariah 9:10 applies it explicitly to the coming king on a donkey. ûminnāhār ʿad-ʾapsê-ʾāreṣ — and from the River to the ends of the earth. The Euphrates (nāhār, the River) marks the northeastern boundary of the Promised Land; ʾapsê-ʾāreṣ (ends of the earth) extends the sovereignty beyond any historical Israelite king's reach — the scope is Messianic.
9: lĕpānāyw yikrĕʿû ṣiyyîm — may the desert creatures bow before him. Ṣiyyîm (desert-dwellers, inhabitants of the uninhabited margins) — even the untamed periphery submits. wĕʾôyĕbāyw ʿāpār yĕlaḥēkû — and his enemies lick the dust. The dust-licking image is ancient Near Eastern treaty language for complete submission and prostration before the suzerain.
10: malkê tarshîsh wĕʾiyyîm minḥāh yāshîbû — the kings of Tarshish and the coastlands render tribute. Tarshish (probably the western Mediterranean, perhaps Spain or Sardinia) represents the furthest west; Sheba and Seba represent the far south. malkê shĕbāʾ wĕsĕbāʾ ʾeshkār yaqrîbû — the kings of Sheba and Seba bring gifts. Matthew's Magi narrative (Matthew 2:11: gold, frankincense, myrrh) draws on this verse — the foreign sages honoring the infant king pattern the eschatological tribute of nations to David's greater Son.
11: wĕyishtaḥăwû-lô kol-mĕlākhîm — may all kings bow down before him. The hithpael of shāḥāh (to prostrate oneself) — the same gesture used of divine worship. Kol-gôyim yaʿăbĕdûhû — all nations serve him. The vision is not conquest but voluntary service — the full reach of the Abrahamic promise (Genesis 12:3) applied to the royal line, anticipating the Great Commission's scope.
12: kî-yaṣṣîl ʾebyôn mĕshawwēaʿ — for he delivers the needy when he cries. The ʾebyôn (the utterly destitute) crying out to the king mirrors Israel's cry to God in Egypt (Exodus 2:23). The ideal king hears and acts as God heard and acted. wĕʿānî wĕʾên-ʿōzēr lô — and the poor who has no helper — complete helplessness is the qualification for royal intervention.
13: yāḥos ʿal-dal wĕʾebyôn — he has pity on the weak and needy. The verb ḥûs (to have compassion, look with tender concern) carries an emotional register — not mere policy but active inward movement toward the suffering. wĕnafshôt ʾebyônîm yôshîaʿ — and saves the lives of the needy. The verb yāshaʿ (save) — the same root as the name Joshua/Jesus.
14: mîtôkh wûmēḥāmās yigʾal nafshām — from oppression and violence he redeems their life. Gāʾal (to act as kinsman-redeemer) is the family-law term for the relative who rescues a family member from debt-slavery or mortal danger. The king functions as the nation's kinsman-redeemer — a role that in Hebrew law belonged to the nearest kin. wĕyîqar dāmām bĕʿênāyw — and their blood is precious in his sight — the value placed on each life is measured by what the king is willing to do to protect it.
15: wîḥî wĕyittenlô mizzahab shĕbāʾ — long may he live; may gold of Sheba be given to him. The life-prayer combined with the tribute of Sheba echoes v10 and anticipates the Magi's gold. wĕyitpallal baʿădô tāmîd — may prayer be made for him continually. The king who prays for his people generates prayer from his people in return — a reciprocal liturgy of intercession.
16: yĕhî phissath-bar bāʾāreṣ — may there be abundance of grain in the land. Phissath-bar (a handful/abundance of grain) — agricultural prosperity as a sign of royal righteousness. The image of grain waving on the mountaintops and growth like the forests of Lebanon images the reversal of curse and the abundance of creation under the righteous king — an Eden-restoration motif.
17: yĕhî shĕmô lĕʿôlām lifnê-shemesh yinnôn shĕmô — may his name endure forever; before the sun his name will propagate. Yinnôn (from nîn: to propagate offspring/posterity) is rare — the name will generate sons, expand, multiply through time. wĕyitbārăkhû bô kol-gôyim — and all nations will be blessed through him. This is the Abrahamic covenant formula (Genesis 12:3; Psalms 18:18; Psalms 22:18; Psalms 26:4; Psalms 28:14) — the Messianic king becomes the mediator of the blessing promised to Abraham. Paul quotes this convergence in Galatians 3:8 as fulfilled in Christ.
18: bārûkh YHWH ʾĕlōhîm ʾĕlōhê yisrāʾêl — blessed be the LORD God, the God of Israel. This doxology (vv18-19) closes Book II of the Psalter (Pss 42-72) — a liturgical hinge between the second Davidic collection and the Asaphite collection. ʿōśēh niplāʾôt lĕbaddô — who alone does wondrous things. The exclusive lĕbaddô (alone) — the wonders of the Messianic kingdom are entirely God's work, not the king's independent achievement.
19: ûbārûkh shēm kĕbôdô lĕʿôlām — and blessed be his glorious name forever. Kābôd (glory, the divine substance and weight) applied to the divine name. wĕyimmālēʾ khĕbôdô ʾet kol-hāʾāreṣ — may all the earth be filled with his glory. The doxological petition echoes the Seraphim's proclamation (Isaiah 6:3) and the Aaronic promise (Numbers 14:21) — the Psalter's vision of the eschatological filling of earth with divine glory.
20: kālû tĕphillôt dāwid ben-yishāy — the prayers of David the son of Jesse are ended. An editorial colophon closing the second Davidic collection (Pss 51-72). Kālû (are finished, completed) — a formal archival note. That Pss 73-89 (the Asaphite collection) follows immediately shows the note marks an editorial unit, not the whole Psalter. The patronymic ben-yishāy (son of Jesse) recalls David's humble origins (1 Samuel 16:11-13) even at the moment his royal prayer-collection is sealed.
